> **Goal:** understand the three factorizations that power machine learning —
> eigendecomposition, the SVD, and PCA — and compute them in NumPy. Builds on the
> vectors and matrices from Chapters 03–04.

## Why a mathematician should care

A matrix is a linear transformation. The deepest way to understand a
transformation is to find the directions it leaves *unchanged in direction* —
the eigenvectors — and how much it stretches them — the eigenvalues. The SVD
extends this to *every* matrix and gives the single most useful tool in applied
linear algebra. PCA then turns the SVD into a practical recipe for finding
structure in data and reducing its dimension. These three ideas reappear
constantly in ML: covariance, embeddings, compression, and more.

## Core ideas in this chapter

### Notebook 05a — Eigenvalues & eigenvectors
- The defining equation $A v = \lambda v$: an **eigenvector** $v$ keeps its
  direction; the **eigenvalue** $\lambda$ is the stretch factor.
- `np.linalg.eig(A)` returns eigenvalues and eigenvectors (eigenvectors are the
  **columns** of the returned matrix).
- **Geometric meaning**, visualized: for an eigen-direction, $Av$ stays on the
  same line; for a generic direction it rotates away.
- **Diagonalization** $A = P D P^{-1}$, with eigenvectors as columns of $P$ and
  eigenvalues on the diagonal of $D$. Powers are easy: $A^k = P D^k P^{-1}$.
- **Symmetric matrices** ($A = A^{\top}$) have **real** eigenvalues and
  **orthogonal** eigenvectors — use `np.linalg.eigh`, and then
  $A = V D V^{\top}$.

### Notebook 05b — SVD, low-rank approximation & PCA
- The **SVD** $A = U \Sigma V^{\top}$ exists for *any* matrix: orthonormal $U$,
  $V$ and non-negative, descending **singular values** $\sigma_i$.
  `np.linalg.svd(A, full_matrices=False)` returns `U, S, Vt`.
- **Low-rank approximation**: keep the top $r$ singular values to get the best
  rank-$r$ approximation (Eckart–Young). The reconstruction error shrinks as $r$
  grows — the basis of compression.
- **PCA from scratch**: center the data, form the covariance matrix
  $C = \frac{1}{N-1} X^{\top} X$, take its eigenvectors (the principal axes),
  project onto the top axis, and report the **variance explained**.
- PCA via the **SVD** of the centered data gives the same axes with no
  covariance matrix.

## Key things that surprise newcomers

| Thing | Note |
|-------|------|
| Eigenvectors are columns | `vecs[:, i]` goes with `vals[i]`, not `vecs[i]` |
| `eig` vs `eigh` | use `eigh` for symmetric matrices: real, orthogonal, faster |
| Complex eigenvalues | a pure rotation has no real eigenvectors |
| `svd` returns `Vt` | it gives $V^{\top}$, already transposed, not $V$ |
| `S` is 1-D | wrap it with `np.diag(S)` before matrix multiplication |
| Center first | PCA needs the data centered, or the axes are meaningless |

## After this chapter

You'll have the linear-algebra machinery — eigenvalues, SVD, and PCA — that
underlies dimensionality reduction, covariance analysis, and many ML models in
the chapters ahead.

## 📚 Further reading

- 3Blue1Brown — [Essence of Linear Algebra](https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLZHQObOWTQDPD3MizzM2xVFitgF8hE_ab) (YouTube) — eigenvectors and eigenvalues visualized.
- [Mathematics for Machine Learning](https://mml-book.com) — Deisenroth, Faisal & Ong (free PDF) — chapters 2–4 on decompositions and PCA.
- Stanford [CS229 lecture notes](https://cs229.stanford.edu/) — PCA and SVD in an ML context.
